1: Standard word-event formula opens the Gog of Magog oracle — the most extensive and most debated oracle in Ezekiel. The entire Ezekiel 38-39 unit is a carefully constructed literary composition: Ezekiel 38 describes the attack, Ezekiel 39 describes the aftermath. The oracle presupposes the restored Israel of Ezekiel 34-37 as its context: the attack comes against a people who have been gathered from the nations and are living in security.
2: Gog of the land of Magog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal — the identity of Gog has been debated since antiquity. (1) The most widely held historical identification is with Gyges of Lydia (d. 644 BCE), known in Akkadian sources as Gu-gu of Luddu — a powerful western Anatolian king who sought Assyrian assistance against northern (Scythian/Cimmerian) invaders. (2) The Scythian identification associates Gog with the Scythian (Greek Skythai) migrations through the Levant in the 7th century BCE, documented by Herodotus and by the prophetic tradition of the "northern foe" in Jeremiah 1:14; Ezekiel 4:6; Ezekiel 6:1, 22. (3) More likely, Gog is a symbolic/eschatological figure — the ultimate enemy of YHWH's people at the end of history — drawing on the northern-enemy tradition without requiring a specific historical referent.
3: YHWH against Gog — the hostile-presence formula (hinneni ʾeleikha) directed at Gog matches the formula used against the nations in Ezekiel 25-32. The oracle is addressed to a figure that YHWH himself summons and turns against Israel (v4), which is the theological key: Gog comes at YHWH's initiative, not merely his own. The attack is YHWH's design, not Gog's independent aggression.
4: "I will turn you about and put hooks in your jaws and bring you out with your whole army" — the hooks-in-jaw imagery inverts the normal direction of divine leading: YHWH leads Israel by the hand; he leads Gog by force, as a captive animal. The image of a captured animal with a hook through its jaw appears in Assyrian reliefs depicting the treatment of conquered kings. YHWH uses the same coercive force on the enemy of his people that the Assyrians used on defeated rulers.
5: The coalition armies: Persia, Cush, and Put — three geographically distinct regions. Persia (Iran) represents the eastern direction; Cush (Ethiopia/Sudan) the southern; Put (Libya) the western. The three represent the compass directions east, south, and west, with Gog's northern coalition providing the northern element (v6). The four-directional scope of the coalition establishes Gog's attack as a truly universal threat from all directions.
6: Gomer and all his hordes, Beth-togarmah from the uttermost parts of the north — Gomer is identified with the Cimmerians (Greek Kimmeroi), a northern steppe people who invaded Anatolia in the 7th century BCE, documented in Assyrian texts. Beth-togarmah is Armenia/southeastern Anatolia. The conjunction of Gomer (Cimmerians) and Meshech/Tubal (peoples of Anatolia and the Black Sea region) in the coalition corresponds to the ANE geography of the "northern peoples" who threatened the civilized world of the ancient Near East.
7: "Be ready and keep ready, you and all your company that are assembled around you, and be a guard for them" — YHWH commanding Gog to prepare is the supreme irony of the oracle: the enemy is being prepared for a battle that will become the instrument of YHWH's self-glorification (v23). Gog's military preparation serves YHWH's purposes, not Gog's. The divine sovereignty over the enemy's preparations is the theological claim that the oracle establishes from the outset.
8: "After many days you will be mustered; in the latter years you will go against the land." — the temporal setting "after many days" (meʾaḥarit yamim) and "latter years" (beʾaḥarit hashanim) places the Gog oracle explicitly in an eschatological timeframe — later than the restoration oracles of Ezekiel 34-37 but still within history. The restored people "gathered from many peoples on the mountains of Israel, which had long been desolate" is the restored community of Ezekiel 34-37 as the target of the attack.
9: "You will come like a storm — like a cloud covering the land." — the cloud-covering image for overwhelming military force appears throughout the prophetic literature for large invading armies (Isaiah 8:7-8; Jeremiah 4:13; Joel 2:2). The storm metaphor also connects to the theophanic storm of YHWH's own approach (Ezekiel 1:4; Nahum 1:3); the enemy comes as a pseudo-theophany, imitating but ultimately confronting the genuine divine storm.
10: Evil thoughts arising in Gog's mind — the internal deliberation of the enemy revealed to the prophet. The three terms for Gog's mental process ("evil thoughts," "devising an evil scheme") expose the motives behind the military campaign: it is an act of rapacity driven by the perception of easy pickings from an undefended, prosperous people. The disclosure of enemy intentions is a feature of prophetic oracles that establishes YHWH's omniscience — he knows Gog's thoughts before Gog acts on them.
11: "I will go up against the land of unwalled villages; I will fall upon the quiet people who dwell securely, all of them dwelling without walls, and having no bars or gates." — the "unwalled villages" (perazot) is the key target-description. Iron Age Canaan had extensive unwalled settlements alongside the fortified cities; after the Babylonian conquest, the rebuilding of fortifications was limited. Gog specifically targets the vulnerability of the restored community — their peace and security (the fulfillment of the new-covenant promise) becomes the occasion for the attack.
12: "To seize spoil and carry off plunder, to turn your hand against the waste places that are now inhabited." — the explicitly economic motivation of Gog's campaign. The restored Israel as a uniquely prosperous and uniquely undefended community is the temptation for the attack. The "navel of the earth" (tabur haʾarets) is a cosmological term for the center of the world — also used in Judges 9:37 for a central geographic location. The concept of Jerusalem/Israel as the world's center (omphalos) appears in later Jewish and Christian cosmography (cf. Ezekiel 5:5, "Jerusalem in the center of the nations").
13: Sheba, Dedan, and the merchants of Tarshish asking whether Gog comes to take spoil — the commercial nations observe the military campaign with their own economic interest. Sheba (southwest Arabia) and Dedan (northwest Arabia) were major caravan trade centers; Tarshish (probably Tartessus in Spain) was the furthest western commercial reach of the Phoenician trading network. Their question ("have you come to seize spoil?") is not opposition but curiosity about whether they can benefit from the campaign — they stand aside as observers rather than participants.
14: "On that day when my people Israel are dwelling securely, will you not know it?" — YHWH's question to Gog is ironic: the security that provokes Gog's attack is YHWH's own gift to the restored community. Gog attacking the secure Israel is therefore attacking not just a human community but YHWH's own covenantal work. The attack is ultimately an attack on YHWH himself, which is why YHWH responds with the full fury of the theophanic battle in Ezekiel 39.
15: Gog coming from his place out of the uttermost parts of the north — the direction "uttermost parts of the north" (yarketei tsafon) is the same phrase used for the divine assembly mountain in Psalms 48:2 (Zion described as the divine assembly mountain in the north, yarkete tsafon) and in Isaiah 14:13 for Baal's assembly mountain. The north in Canaanite cosmography was the direction of the divine assembly; Gog comes from the direction associated with the divine realm — a final counterfeit of the divine warrior theme.
16: "You will come up against my people Israel, like a cloud covering the land. In the latter days I will bring you against my land" — YHWH explicitly claims the initiative: "I will bring you against my land" (ʾavi'akha ʾal-ʾartsiy). The land is YHWH's (ʾartsiy, my land), not merely Israel's; the attack on it is therefore an attack on YHWH's own property. The purpose — "that the nations may know me when through you, O Gog, I vindicate my holiness before their eyes" — reveals that YHWH brings Gog for self-vindicating purposes.
17: "Are you he of whom I spoke in former days by my servants the prophets of Israel?" — YHWH's rhetorical identification of Gog with the northern-enemy tradition of the earlier prophets. This connects the Gog oracle to Jeremiah's "foe from the north" oracles (Jeremiah 1:14; Ezekiel 4:6; Ezekiel 6:1, 22) and possibly to Amos 5:27 and other northern-enemy threats. The Gog oracle is presented as the fulfillment of the entire northern-enemy prophetic tradition — all the prior prophecies of the northern enemy find their ultimate expression in this eschatological assault.
18: "On that day when Gog comes against the land of Israel, my wrath will be roused in my anger." — the divine anger at the attack on the restored community. The three-part anger vocabulary (ḥamati + ʾappi + qinʾati, wrath + anger + jealousy) expresses the superlative intensity of YHWH's response. The fire of YHWH's jealousy is the same qinʾah that drove the restoration "for my name's sake" in Ezekiel 36:5 — it is now directed in defense of the restored people.
19: "In my jealousy and in my blazing wrath I declare: On that day there shall be a great earthquake in the land of Israel." — the cosmic earthquake as the vehicle of divine judgment. ANE theophany traditions consistently included earthquake as a component of the divine appearance (1 Kings 19:11-12; Psalms 68:7-8; Isaiah 29:6; Joel 2:10; Amos 1:1; Nahum 1:5). The earthquake in this oracle is not merely natural but theophanic — the earth responds to YHWH's presence as it did at Sinai (Exodus 19:18).
20: The shaking of fish, birds, beasts, creeping things, and all people — the totality of creation responding to the divine earthquake. The comprehensive list of living beings (fish of the sea, birds of the sky, beasts of the field, creeping things, all people on the face of the earth) mirrors the creation-taxonomy of Gen 1. The total-creation response to the divine earthquake is a de-creation moment: the created order is shaken to its foundations by YHWH's direct intervention in history.
21: Every sword turned against his brother — the pan-military confusion that destroys the attacking coalition from within. Mutual destruction within the enemy coalition is a recurring motif in YHWH's holy war: Gideon's army of 300 routing the Midianite coalition through their own mutual slaughter (Judges 7:22); Jehoshaphat's victory through the enemy turning on itself (2 Chronicles 20:23). YHWH fighting for Israel against the nations without Israel needing to fight is the consistent pattern of the holy-war tradition.
22: Pestilence, blood, torrential rain, hailstones, fire, and sulfur — the arsenal of covenant-curse weapons directed at Gog. Hail and fire appear in the Egyptian plague narrative (Exodus 9:23-24); the sulfur-and-fire vocabulary echoes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24) and the theophanic battle in Psalms 11:6. The pestilence and blood are the siege-weapons used against Jerusalem in Ezekiel 5-6 — now directed outward against the enemy of the restored community.
23: "I will show my greatness and my holiness and make myself known in the eyes of many nations. Then they will know that I am YHWH." — the ultimate purpose of the Gog oracle and the climax of the entire Ezekiel book's "that they will know that I am YHWH" motif. The defeat of Gog is the final, universal recognition event — not just Israel or the surrounding nations but "many nations" see YHWH's greatness and holiness vindicated in the defeat of the ultimate eschatological enemy.
